title: History’s hockey stick: Worldwide historical real gross domestic product per capita
subtitle: Unit 1 'The capitalist revolution: prosperity, inequality, and planetary limits’ in The CORE Team, The Economy 2.0 Microeconomics. Available at: https://tinyco.re/19274920 [Figure 1.1]
caption:
History’s hockey stick: Worldwide historical real gross domestic product per capita

Unit 1 'The capitalist revolution: prosperity, inequality, and planetary limits’ in The CORE Team, The Economy 2.0 Microeconomics. Available at: https://tinyco.re/19274920 [Figure 1.1]

The chart plots real gross domestic product per capita for five countries — Japan, the United Kingdom, Italy, China, and India — from the year 1000 to 2018, with values expressed in 2011 US dollars adjusted for Purchasing Power Parity. From 1000 through approximately 1700, all five lines remain clustered near the bottom of the vertical axis, mostly below $2,000, with minor fluctuations visible for Italy and the United Kingdom between 1300 and 1600. Beginning around 1750, the United Kingdom’s line begins a gradual ascent, crossing $5,000 by 1850 and accelerating sharply after 1900 to reach nearly $40,000 by 2018. Japan’s trajectory remains flat until the late 1800s, then rises steeply after 1950, surpassing $35,000 by 2018. Italy follows a similar pattern to the United Kingdom but at slightly lower levels, peaking just under $35,000. China and India show minimal growth until the mid-20th century; China’s line climbs rapidly after 1980, reaching approximately $15,000 by 2018, while India’s rise begins later and reaches about $7,000 by the end of the period. The visual structure shows prolonged stagnation followed by abrupt upward inflections, particularly pronounced for Japan and the United Kingdom in the 20th century. All values are measured in constant 2011 US dollars to enable cross-country and cross-time comparisons of economic output per person.
